Journal.Title: Journal of orthopaedic surgery and research

Article.ArticleTitle: Alternative alignment clinical study: study protocol for a prospective, randomised, controlled study of the outcomes and cost effectiveness of triathlon CR knee replacement system: traditional philosophy versus alternative alignment philosophy.

Abstract.AbstractText: Despite being one of the most successful orthopaedic operation, a number of patients remain dissatisfied after TKR surgery. There has been a recent move by some to change surgical philosophy to aim to replicate the alignment of the patient's own original knee-known as kinematic or alternative alignment. The purpose of this study is identify if using the alternative alignment technique can lead to significantly improved outcomes when comparted to the traditional alignment technique.

A single centre, randomised control trial, powered to recruit 126 patients (63 in each arm). The primary aim is to identify if the group undergoing TKR using the alternative alignment technique have a significantly improved functional outcome at 1 year when assessed by the minimal clinically important difference of at least 5 points in the Oxford knee score by comparison to the group using traditional alignment technique. Various other secondary outcomes are set out in the main text.

A study examining short-, medium- and longer-term outcomes of alternative alignment is needed to see if outcomes for patients can be improved. This study has the potential to be of great importance in contributing to this question. Limb alignment has focussed on the need to achieve the zero-degree goal of hip, knee and ankle alignment. To achieve real 3D more natural alignment, surgeons have to accept this goal may not be correct. There is a need to achieve pre-arthritic/pre-morbid alignment and to evaluate if this can unlock the potential for improved patient outcomes. A prospective, randomised study with multiple outcome measures can deliver a major contribution to the knowledge base of this subject and influence surgical practice with the aim of improving patient outcomes and satisfaction.
The study was approved by the Wales Research Ethics Committee (IRAS project ID: 225871), and was registered with clinicaltrials.gov (Identifier NCT03196011).

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: University Hospitals Plymouth and University of Exeter, Exeter, UK. jonathon.sheen@nhs.net.
Princess Elizabeth Orthopaedic Centre, Royal Devon and Exeter Hospital, Exeter, UK.
Princess Elizabeth Orthopaedic Centre, Royal Devon and Exeter Hospital, Exeter, UK.
Princess Elizabeth Orthopaedic Centre, Royal Devon and Exeter Hospital, Exeter, UK.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Ethics approval and consent to participate: The study was approved by the Wales Research Ethics Committee (IRAS project ID: 225871), and was registered with clinicaltrials.gov (Identifier NCT03196011). The study coordinator has discussed the study with the radiological protection officer (HOB) at the RD + E Hospital who has provided radiation dose calculations as follows: Patients undergoing total knee replacement operations will receive AP and Lateral radiographs of the knee on 5 occasions (pre & post op, at 1, 2 years and 5 years). Patients taking part in this study will receive 2 additional AP radiographs of both legs at pre-op & 6 weeks post operatively. The total dose arising from these examinations is 81µSv (HPA-CRCE-012, 2010). In addition, patients enrolled in this trial will require 2 additional whole leg AP X-rays and 2 CT scans covering the hip, knee and ankle. These CT scans will result in an additional dose of 4 mSv per scan. The total dose that a patient participating in this trial will receive is 8.1 mSv, of which 8 mSv is in addition to standard care. Participants are ≥ 50 years of age and pregnant women are excluded from the trial. Ionising radiation can cause cancer which manifests itself after many years or decades. The lifetime risk of developing a cancer resulting from the total radiation dose is 1 in 3200 for a 50-year-old patient, 1 in 4,600 for a 60-year-old patient; this would fall to 1 in 18,900 for an 80-year-old patient. For the ages of the expected cohort, this is considered to be a low risk (HPA-CRCE-028, 2011 and NRPB, 2001) and is equivalent to around 3.2 years of natural background radiation in the UK. The lifetime risk of developing a cancer resulting from the excess dose in this trial is 1 in 3200 for a 50-year-old patient, 1 in 4,700 for a 60-year-old patient; this would fall to 1 in 19,100 for an 80-year-old patient. For the ages of the expected cohort, this is considered to be a low risk (HPA-CRCE-028, 2011 and NRPB, 2001) and is equivalent to around 3.2 years of natural background radiation in the UK. With participants consent, we will send identifiable data regarding the CT scan and X-ray images to LifeIMAGE for forwarding to Stryker US. This will be with the purpose of providing information to allow Stryker US to make the individual surgical plan. We have considered the possibility of sending non-identifiable data to Stryker via LifeIMAGE. However, we strongly feel that this has the potential to introduce a degree of risk that is unacceptable. We have concerns that our normal practice of ensuring patient identification would be compromised if we use a study number only. It is normal practice at the beginning of an operation to perform what is called a ‘time out’ procedure. This is a 2-way process between the surgeon and the anaesthetist using patient name, hospital number and date of birth to ensure the correct plan has been attributed to the correct patient before surgery starts. Identifying the MAKO individually tailored plan by study ID number only would lessen the rigour with which we normally ensure correct patient identification. One method of identification is not enough when the surgical plan is completely unique. Data sent to LifeIMAGE and Stryker US will be via secure internet connections, encrypted memory sticks and managed and disposed of as per protocol. Competing interests: Stryker UK have supported this study financially. However, it remains completely independent from them in study design, results and dissemination of findings. ADT, JRP, VIM and KSE all undertake consultancy work for Stryker UK and will complete the necessary applications regarding this on the ethics application for this study. To ensure continued impartiality, these operating consultants are not involved in the data collection or analysis. Confidentiality: All of the research staff will be GCP trained. All NHS staff involved are bound by patient confidentiality requirements of the NHS. The study participants GP will be informed that their patient is involved in the research- particularly as the patient may wish to discuss their involvement in the research with their GP. With the exception of the information collected (1) through the Instride Accelerometer, (2) the 21 day accelerometer data collated by the University of Exeter, and (3) the surgical planning data, all of the data associated with the study will be kept electronically on the hard drives of computers within the Exeter Knee Reconstruction Unit (EKRU). It will be stored on encrypted and password protected hospital computers. The data will only be accessible by members of the research team and by the R + D department at the RD + E Hospital who will require access to the data to ensure compliance with the protocol and that we are meeting all study regulatory requirements. Re the InStride accelerometer: For clinical patients, they will first access the InStride app and web portal with their clinician (physiotherapist or surgeon) and be directly on boarded to InStride. The onboarding includes providing their unique study number as the only means of identification. This contains no personal data. It is entered by the clinician into the InStride web portal. The InStride user provides their unique study number to an authorized clinician for entry into the InStride web portal to generate a specific user profile. The authorized clinician generates a token (unique code) for that user profile that is then entered into the InStride app. The token provides the secure match between the user profile and the Smartphone. The user’s walk pattern is compared only to their own baseline (set as a 30 or 40 m straight line walk in the presence of their clinician) and does not mingle with any other users’ patterns. InStride is approved for use at Nova Scotia Health Authority and has passed both ethics board review and privacy impact assessment. InStride adheres to the following policy guidelines: Nova Scotia Personal Health Information Act, Policy CH 07–015 Access, Use and Disclosure of Personal Health Information, and Policy CH 30 -100 Privacy and Confidentiality of Personal Health Information. InStride patient user profiles will contain only the participants’ unique study number and their baseline walk patterns. Also stored with their profile is the history of saved walking patterns as well as the unprocessed triaxial accelerometer data. These profiles are securely stored on an Azure server in Montreal Canada with SSL encryption security and access through password-controlled web portal at www.instrideweb.com . Only the patient themselves and their authorized clinical team can access their InStride profile. Each clinical team is established with secure access only to the patients they onboard to InStride. The University of Exeter accelerometer data will be stored on password protected University of Exeter computers with all personal identifiers removed and only the study number used for identification. When the data is sent for statistical analysis, all personal identifiers will be removed and the study number only used for identification. Personal identifiers (patient name, hospital number and date of birth) will be used when information about the CT scan and X-ray findings are sent to LifeIMAGE and Stryker US. At the end of the study (see timescales above), all research data will be archived as per our sponsors archiving SOP. Radiographs and scans will remain on the secure hospital systems as per routine care.

Reference.Citation: Noble PC, Conditt MA, Cook KF, Mathis KB. Patient expectations affect satisfaction with total knee arthroplasty. Clin Orthop Relat Res. 2006;452:35–43. 10.1097/01.blo.0000238825.63648.1e.

Brin YS, Nikolaou VS, Joseph L, Zukor DJ, Antoniou J. Imageless computer-assisted versus conventional total knee replacement: a Bayesian meta-analysis of 23 comparative studies. Int Orthop. 2011;35:331–9. 10.1007/s00264-010-1008-6.

Parratte S, Pagnano MW, Trousdale RT, Berry DJ. Effect of postoperative mechanical axis alignment on the fifteen-year survival of modern, cemented total knee replacements. J Bone Joint Surg Am. 2010;92:2143–9. 10.2106/JBJS.I.01398.

Bellemans J, Colyn W, Vandenneucker H, Victor J. Is neutral mechanical alignment normal for all patients? The concept of constitutional varus. Clin Orthop Relat Res. 2012;470:45–53. 10.1007/s11999-011-1936-5.

Bonner TJ, Eardley WGP, Patterson P, Gregg PJ. The effect of post-operative mechanical axis alignment on the survival of primary total knee replacements after a follow-up of 15 years. J Bone Joint Surg Br. 2011;93:1217–22. 10.1302/0301-620X.93B9.26249.
Waterson HB, Phillips JRA, Mandalia VI, Toms AD. ‘Thou shalt not commit varus’: still applicable in total knee arthroplasty? Bone Joint 360. 2014;3(3):16–21. 10.1302/2048-0105.33.360227.

Waterson HB, Clement ND, Eyres KS, Mandalia VI, Toms AD. The early outcome of kinematic versus mechanical alignment in total knee arthroplasty: a prospective randomised control trial. Bone Joint J. 2016;98:1360–8. 10.1302/0301-620X.98B10.38047.

Mizner RL, Stevens JE, Snyder-Mackler L. Voluntary activation and decreased force production of the quadriceps femoris muscle after total knee arthroplasty. Phys Ther. 2003;83:359–65. 10.1093/ptj/83.4.359.
Migliorini F, Maffulli N, Pilone M, et al. Mechanical versus kinematic alignment for total knee arthroplasty: a meta-analysis. Arch Orthop Trauma Surg. 2025;145(1):212. 10.1007/s00402-025-05835-7.
Bade MJ, Kohrt WM, Stevens-Lapsley JE. Outcomes before and after total knee arthroplasty compared to healthy adults. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 2010;40:559–67. 10.2519/jospt.2010.3317.
Dossett HG, Swartz GJ, Estrada NA, Lefevre GW, Kwasman BG. Kinematically versus mechanically aligned total knee arthroplasty. Orthopedics. 2012;35:e160–9. 10.3928/01477447-20120123-04.
Chan AW, Tetzlaff JM, Gøtzsche PC, et al. SPIRIT 2013 explanation and elaboration: guidance for protocols of clinical trials. BMJ. 2013;346:e7586. 10.1136/bmj.e7586.

Clement ND, MacDonald D, Simpson AH. The minimal clinically important difference in the Oxford knee score and Short Form 12 score after total knee arthroplasty. Knee Surg Sports Traumatol Arthrosc. 2014;22:1933–9. 10.1007/s00167-013-2776-5.
